flashcards.front: What is a stream processing event, and how does stream processing differ from batch processing?
flashcards.back: An event is a small, self-contained, immutable object describing something that happened at a point in time (user action, sensor reading, database write). Unlike batch processing (runs on bounded input files periodically), stream processing handles unbounded, continuously arriving events in real time. Key difference: batch = read input file, produce output file, repeat; stream = react to events as they arrive, produce continuous output. In streaming: events are produced by a producer/publisher, grouped into topics/streams, consumed by multiple consumers/subscribers. Low-latency: instead of polling (expensive), consumers are notified when new events appear.

flashcards.front: What is a message broker, and how does it differ from a database?
flashcards.back: A message broker (message queue) is a server-based intermediary: producers write messages to it, consumers receive messages from it. Brokers tolerate clients going offline — durability moves to the broker. Key differences from databases: (1) Brokers auto-delete messages after delivery — not meant for permanent storage; databases keep data until explicitly deleted. (2) Brokers assume small working set — slow consumers can cause performance degradation as queues grow; databases handle large datasets natively. (3) Brokers notify clients when new data arrives; databases return point-in-time snapshots. (4) Brokers support topic subscriptions; databases support secondary indexes and arbitrary queries. Multiple consumer patterns: Load balancing — each message to one consumer (share processing work); Fan-out — each message to all consumers (multiple independent views of same data). Implemented by: RabbitMQ, ActiveMQ, IBM MQ, Azure Service Bus (AMQP/JMS protocol).

flashcards.front: How does Kafka's log-based message broker work? How does it differ from traditional AMQP brokers?
flashcards.back: Kafka architecture: log-based append-only storage. Topics are partitioned; each partition is an append-only log on disk. Messages get a monotonically increasing offset per partition. Producers append to end; consumers read sequentially from their last offset. Consumer groups: each partition assigned to one consumer in a group — partition is the unit of parallelism (max consumers = num partitions). Key differences from AMQP: (1) Messages not deleted after consumption — consumers track their own offset. A slow consumer doesn't disrupt others. (2) Replay: rewind offset to reprocess past events — impossible in AMQP (ack deletes message). (3) Log is durably persisted to disk — message throughput stays constant regardless of queue depth (vs AMQP which slows down when spilling to disk). (4) Fan-out free — multiple consumer groups each get all messages independently. (5) Ordering guaranteed within a partition; no cross-partition ordering. Kafka throughput: millions of messages/sec via partitioning across machines. Log retention: disk-bounded circular buffer — typically days to weeks of history. Used in: LinkedIn (origin), Uber, Netflix, Confluent.

flashcards.front: What is Change Data Capture (CDC) and how does it differ from Event Sourcing?
flashcards.back: Change Data Capture (CDC): extracting changes from a database's replication log and streaming them to other systems (search indexes, caches, data warehouses) to keep them in sync. The database is the system of record; CDC makes its changes available as a stream. Implementation: parse MySQL binlog, PostgreSQL logical replication, Debezium, Kafka Connect. Benefit: avoids dual-write bugs (writing to DB + cache atomically) — instead derive the cache from the DB change stream. Event Sourcing: application stores all state changes as an immutable log of events (events are first-class, the application explicitly appends them). Current state is derived by replaying events. CDC is retrofitted (extract changes from DB); Event Sourcing is designed-in (events are the source of truth). Shared idea (log as truth): both treat the log as the authoritative record. State is derived from the log, not the other way around. Analogy: accounting ledger (append debits/credits) → account balance (derived). Git (commits = events) → working tree (derived state).

flashcards.front: What are the three types of stream joins? What is the challenge of time in stream processing (event time vs processing time)?
flashcards.back: Three stream join types: (1) Stream-stream join (windowed): join events from two streams that occurred within the same time window. Example: correlate search queries with ad clicks within 1 hour. Both streams must be buffered in state for the window duration. (2) Stream-table join (stream enrichment): enrich a stream event with data from a slowly-changing database table. Load table into operator state; look up on each event. (3) Table-table join (materialized view maintenance): both inputs are change streams from databases; maintain a materialized join view that updates as either table changes. Time challenge — event time vs processing time: Event time: when the event actually occurred (timestamp in the event). Processing time: when the event was processed by the stream processor. They diverge due to: network delays, device clock skew, offline devices sending batched events later. Solution: watermarks — a heuristic assertion that all events with timestamp < T have been received. Allows closing windows and emitting results. Late events after watermark: either drop them, update results (requires downstream to handle corrections), or use a long enough window to absorb expected delays.

flashcards.front: How do stream processors achieve fault tolerance and exactly-once semantics?
flashcards.back: Challenge: stream processing must tolerate failures without losing or duplicating events. Delivery guarantees: At-most-once: process and move on; if failure, lost (lowest overhead). At-least-once: retry on failure — events may be processed multiple times. Exactly-once: hardest — every event processed exactly once even under failures. Achieving exactly-once: (1) Idempotent operations: design processing so re-applying an event produces the same result (no harm from re-execution). Example: SET balance = 100 is idempotent; ADD 10 to balance is not. (2) Atomic commit: commit processed output AND consumer offset atomically (Kafka transactions). If output commits but offset doesn't → reprocess → but if idempotent, no harm. (3) Micro-batching (Spark Streaming): break stream into small batches (e.g., 1-second intervals), process as batch jobs with batch fault-tolerance guarantees. Exactly-once per micro-batch; latency = batch size. (4) Checkpointing (Apache Flink): periodically snapshot all operator state to durable storage. On failure: restart from last checkpoint, replay events since checkpoint offset. Flink achieves true exactly-once with Chandy-Lamport distributed snapshots. Trade-off: higher exactly-once overhead vs. lower at-least-once overhead. For most analytics (counting, averaging), at-least-once + idempotent aggregations is sufficient. For financial operations, exactly-once is required.

puzzles.title: Log-Based vs Traditional Broker Properties
puzzles.instructions: Match each property to: Log-based broker (Kafka), Traditional broker (RabbitMQ/AMQP), or Both.

pairs.left: Messages deleted after consumer acknowledges them
Consumers can replay events by rewinding their offset
pairs.right: Log-based (Kafka) — offset is consumer-controlled, log is immutable

pairs.left: Multiple consumer groups each receive all messages independently
pairs.right: Log-based (Kafka) — each group reads independently without affecting others

pairs.left: Producer writes, consumer reads, broker decouples them
pairs.right: Both — this is the fundamental broker model

puzzles.hint: The key Kafka insight: consuming is read-only. The key AMQP insight: consuming is destructive.
puzzles.explanation: Kafka's append-only log enables replay and independent consumer groups. AMQP's ack-and-delete model is simpler for task queues but prevents replay and slows under load.

puzzles.title: Change Data Capture Pipeline
puzzles.instructions: Arrange the components of a CDC pipeline that keeps a search index in sync with a PostgreSQL database.

steps.text: Application writes an order to PostgreSQL (system of record)
PostgreSQL records the write in its logical replication log (WAL)
Debezium reads the WAL and publishes change events to a Kafka topic
Elasticsearch consumer reads from the Kafka topic
Consumer updates the Elasticsearch index with the new order data
Users can now search for the order in Elasticsearch (with small lag)

puzzles.hint: The WAL is the source; Kafka is the transport; the consumer drives the derived system.
puzzles.explanation: CDC pipelines: DB → WAL → CDC tool (Debezium) → Kafka → downstream consumers. This avoids dual-write bugs — the Kafka stream is derived from the single authoritative DB write.
